coredata.dc:description: Plastic pollution, the energy crisis, and climate change are significant global challenges that threaten human sustainability and social development. Additionally, addressing pollution while simultaneously promoting valorization techniques for the development of effective personal protective equipment to mitigate the transmission of the SARS-CoV-2 virus poses a challenge, particularly in maintaining wearer comfort. Current advancements in intelligent future therapies focus on the incorporation of quantum nanostructures with theranostic capabilities that are compatible with the skin, reduce wear interference, and facilitate easy integration into minimally invasive surgical procedures. To address these challenges, we propose a win–win strategy that enables microplasma technology and high-throughput electrospinning technology to prepare sustainable self-powered angiogenesis inspired ultrafine nanofibers (AINFs). The proposed quantum nanostructure-anchored AINFs are designed to support the development of flex-insensitive white light-emitting optoelectronics (92 % at 500 cycles), COVID-19 face masks of record high-quality factors (0.167 Pa−1 @ PM0.2), and highly compatible large-scale self-powered theranostic capabilities (2694 pmV−1). These innovations align with the urgent demands of a circular economy and foster environmentally sustainable applications within the Internet of Medical Things.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of cellulose acetate nanofiber (CNF), graphene quantum dot nanofiber (GQNF), and angiogenesis inspired nanofibers (AINF). (a) Schematic representation for the preparation of win–win strategy enabled cellulose nanofiber (CNF), graphene quantum dot nanofibers (GQNF), and angiogenesis inspired nanofibers (AINF). (b) Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) of AINF. (c) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of AINF. (d) Photoluminescent (PL) spectra. (e) PL quantum yield of GQNFs and AINF. (f) Confocal microscopic image of AINF. (g) Surface tension and solution conductivity of GQNF and AINF. (h) Thermogravimetric thermal stability profile and (i) Tensile stress vs strain plot of CNF, GQNF and AINF.

Light emitting properties of the as-prepared nanofibers. (a) Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curve. (b) PL stability of AINF against deionized (DI) water. (c) PL stability of AINF against phosphate buffer saline solution (PBS). (d) White light emitting diode (WLED) device architecture. (e) Luminance vs voltage plot. (f) CIE plot of as-formed WLED. (g) Electroluminescence spectra. (h) Nanostructured materials degradation studies in PBS solvent and (i) Flexural fatigue test of GQNF and AINF based WLED.

Face masks preliminary simulation and experimental results, along with its real-time operational stability. (a) Geodict facemask simulation studies on CNF, GQNF and AINF based filters, (i) Morphological evolution, (ii) Particle filtration quality, and (iii) Pressure drop significance. (b) Particle size dependent filtration efficiency (Simulation results). (c) Pressure drop and filtration efficiency variations (Simulation results). (d) Pressure drop stability (Simulation results). (e) Filtration efficiency and Pressure drop dependency with respect to AINF’s basis weight. (f) Infrared thermal profile variation against commercial and AINF based facemasks upon inhaling and exhaling. (g) Simulation filtration results and calculated quality factor. (h) Experimental filtration results and calculated quality factor. (i) Real time as-fabricated AINF based facemask. (j) Filtration efficiency durability studies. (k) Pressure drop durability studies and (l) Washing stability of the as-prepared facemasks.

Self-powered piezoelectric wearable device performance and its potential in in vitro cell proliferation and wound recoverability. (a) Self-powered piezoelectric energy generator architecture. (b) Voltage characteristics upon cyclic loading–unloading process. (c) Voltage reversal phenomena on forward and reverse bias connection. (d) Frequency dependent voltage outputs. (e) AING durability studies (f) AING storage stability studies. (f) Cell proliferation studies (left: control, right: AING). (h) Quantification of cell proliferation rate (Inset: AING device in cell proliferation) and (i) Cell migration test derived simulated wound recovery characteristics (Inset: AING device in cell migration).

Real time large scale deep tissue wound recovery studies based on minimally invasive AINF approach. (a) Schematic illustration of the real time wound healing in humans and AING significance. (b) Large area tissue regenerative progress in Sprague Dawley (SD) rats. (c) Histopathological analysis − hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. (d) Masson’s trichrome staining. Quantitative verification of (e) epidermal thickness (f) Hair follicle counts, and (g) collagen deposition after the wound recovery process (28 days).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Angiogenesis inspired ultrafine nanofibers based intelligent self-powered personal protective textiles derived sustainably through win–win strategies via successful exploitation of microplasma plastic valorization and high-throughput electrospinning technology. Bio-mimicked nano-anchored ultrafine air permeable structures, have contributed significantly to the achievement of flex-insensitive white light optoelectronics, a high-quality factor recording robust antibacterial COVID-19 masks, and sustainable self-powered low-cost and minimally invasive wearable theranostics.

Plastic pollution, the energy crisis, and climate change are significant global challenges that threaten human sustainability and social development. Additionally, addressing pollution while simultaneously promoting valorization techniques for the development of effective personal protective equipment to mitigate the transmission of the SARS-CoV-2 virus poses a challenge, particularly in maintaining wearer comfort. Current advancements in intelligent future therapies focus on the incorporation of quantum nanostructures with theranostic capabilities that are compatible with the skin, reduce wear interference, and facilitate easy integration into minimally invasive surgical procedures. To address these challenges, we propose a win–win strategy that enables microplasma technology and high-throughput electrospinning technology to prepare sustainable self-powered angiogenesis inspired ultrafine nanofibers (AINFs). The proposed quantum nanostructure-anchored AINFs are designed to support the development of flex-insensitive white light-emitting optoelectronics (92 % at 500 cycles), COVID-19 face masks of record high-quality factors (0.167 Pa−1 @ PM0.2), and highly compatible large-scale self-powered theranostic capabilities (2694 pmV−1). These innovations align with the urgent demands of a circular economy and foster environmentally sustainable applications within the Internet of Medical Things.

ce:keyword.ce:text: National Institute of occupational Safety and Health

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In human evolution over several centuries, nature offers greater hierarchical structures and multifunctional materials that govern the excellent performance over simple manmade architectures [1–3]. In short, futuristic vision on complex human skin and bio mimicked structures with unique and astonishing properties, such as multifunctional sensory excellence, high resistance and rigidity, lead to architecture inspired by nature for the technology driven materials-based wearable electronics and optoelectronics [4–7]. Furthermore, the imitation of such highly beneficial micro-nanostructures in synthetic/natural composites has led to advances in the creation of bio-inspired electronic skins [8–10]. In particular, the natural angiogenesis process is a well-known phenomenon in living organisms and is closely interdependent with many factors such as control of bacterial infections, the microenvironment of wound inflammation, phenotype, extracellular matrix proteins [11–14]. The achievement of economically viable angiogenesis-inspired nanofiber structures will be of greater importance in quantum nanostructure enabled self-powered smart theranostics due to their interpenetrating, highly entangled, mechanically robust, air permeable and lightweight properties.

Among plastics, polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is a cheap and popular synthetic polymer that produces about 30 million tons annually for various applications, including more than 400 billion bottle of drinks [15]. The accumulation of PET waste presents significant environmental challenges. Although microbiologists and biotechnologists have created enzymes and microbes capable of degrading plastics, the process is complicated by issues related to processing, activation, storage, and environmental conditions [16 17]. Unfortunately, available technologies have partially failed to balance waste plastic recycling due to processing difficulties, environmental considerations and the demanding economics [18–20]. Undoubtedly, the mechanically recycled PET pose significant challenges in attaining the high-performance applications, due to their retarded mechanical properties caused by the recycling processes. Disturbingly, the combustion of one ton PET can release 2300 kg CO2 [21]. Aside from combustion, the depolymerization process of waste PET in value-added products for high-end applications promotes the large-scale removal of PET plastics from the environment [22,23]. Changing the path to the preparation of functional graphene quantum dots with excellent optical and electrical properties can be a game changer in the valorization utilities rather than competing with the mechanical robustness imbalance during conventional processing methods.

After the COVID-19 outbreak, face masks were recommended by healthcare industries and scientific society as an affordable personal protection equipment option to control the viral transmission and propagation against SARS-CoV-2 and its variants (including wild, alpha, delta, and omicron). Besides, from the personal safety solutions, it is of crucial tasks to manage, recycle or upcycle the single-use plastic-based face masks, which seriously pollute the environment and constitute a strong competitive threat to marine, soil and human resources [24–26]. Recent estimates indicate that the substitution of melt-blown microfibers with laser-assisted nanofibers has the potential to decrease polypropylene plastic consumption by approximately 920,000 tonnes each year in face mask manufacturing [27]. In addition to microplastic pollution, microplastics and nanoplastics exacerbate environmental challenges, underscoring the critical necessity for sustainable and eco-friendly micro- and nanofibers that offer improved biocompatibility and biodegradability [28]. A primary challenge is the development of natural nanofibrous membranes that achieve enhanced filtration efficiency and reduced pressure drop, thereby improving both the mask’s performance and sustainability [29–31]. Incorporating sensory functions together with self-sustainability without compromising air permeability, stability and durability of reusable theranostics adds value to the development of 6G-enabled Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) applications [32–34].

Recent advancements in wound dressing technologies such as films, hydrocolloids, scaffolds, foams, hydrogels, and electrospun nanofibers demonstrate enhanced flexibility and elasticity compared to conventional dry gauze and cotton. However, the necessity for frequent dressing changes can impede the healing process in diabetic wounds. Recent studies have investigated innovative methods, including the integration of therapeutic fillers, functional nanoparticles, and external stimuli (such as photothermal and electric fields) to promote healing [35–38]. Despite their promise, these strategies encounter challenges related to operational range, degradation, compatibility, and energy requirements [39,40]. Current therapeutic solutions predominantly depend on traditional power sources, underscoring the urgent need for sustainable, self-powered micro devices that can safely degrade, thereby fostering a circular economy and supporting global net-zero emission objectives. These rapid biodegradable/absorbable, dissolvable on-demand or painlessly removable battery free IoMTs, possibly curtails the frequent implant/removal steps and granting medical comfort to the patients. Currently, implantable, ingestible and in-vivo therapeutics based existing tissue regenerative models limits the user comfort, stimulation time, handling complications, removal and tissue recovery factors. On the other hand, the demand for wearable and implantable smart tactile electronics continues to rise, thereby pushing the development of multifunctional, biodegradable, and sustainable smart theranostic devices with special emphasis on health monitoring, human–machine interfaces and IoMT applications.

This study explores the development of environmentally friendly white light-emitting materials utilizing graphene quantum dots in ecofriendly water solvents. The development of filler-infused electrospun cellulose polymers has led to quantum nanostructural angiogenesis-inspired ultrafine nanofibers (AINF), which are eco-friendly, sustainable, scalable, and lightweight, making them ideal for sustainable wearable electronics. Compared to traditional nanofibers, AINF exerts nano-anchoring and effective physico-chemical entanglements that promote mechanical robustness and integrity, imbue them with flex-insensitive white light emitting optoelectronics. The as-proposed facilely tuned win–win strategy enabled highly scalable AINF robust nano-frameworks reveals its potential in personal protective face masks with excellent quality factor, which is verified via simulation and experimental approach. Furthermore, the achievement of near unity crystallinic alignments favored constructively in achieving the higher piezoelectric coefficients that supports the self-powered theranostics. Finally, we demonstrated a straightforward in vivo model composed of self-powered, highly comfortable minimally invasive point of care tremor diagnostics and chronic large-scale subcutaneous wound tissue regeneration.

Fig. 1 a shows the microplasma-enabled polyethylene terephthalate (PET) plastic valorization process. This process allows the formation of white-emitting graphene quantum dots (GQD) functional materials. The entire process of microplasma synthesis, parameters and optimization are described in the experimental section. The inset in Fig. 1a illustrates the white light emission characteristics of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) under UV light at 365 nm, thereby confirming the successful synthesis of white-emissive GQDs. These GQDs are then homogeneously integrated with cellulose acetate (CA) polymer to create nanofibers via an electrospinning process. The resulting graphene quantum dot nanofibers (GQNFs) are produced in a series with different GQD concentrations, identified as GQNFx (where x = 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, and 12.5). This systematic variation facilitates the precise tuning of optical properties, making them suitable for potential applications in light-emitting devices and other smart wearable applications. The first phase of GQD mix optimization was done with images of scanning electron microscopic (SEM) shown in Fig. S1. Controlled doping (2 wt%) of surface modifier – cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) together with optimized ionically conductive GQDs relatively finely adjusts the morphological aspects to form the angiogenesis inspired nanofibers (AINF). AINF morphology remains unique with an exceptionally low average diameter (71.31 ± 54.76 nm) and a narrow nanofiber distribution area than the control nanofibers of cellulose nanofiber (CNF) (508.07 ± 22.97 nm) and GQNF 7.5 (192.48 ± 45.93 nm) (Fig. 1b, S1& S2). Considering optical applications, it is highly desirable to improve optical stability by improving the functional material dispersion, quantum confinement, and deposition resistance. The transmission electron microscopic image illustrates the consistent distribution of GQDs within the nanofiber matrix of the AINF structure. Furthermore, the inset histogram indicates a narrow and uniform size distribution, with values ranging from 1 to 3 nm (Fig. 1c). It is hypothesized that hierarchical nanofiber splitting will give rise to excellent dispersion and quantum confinement, leading to excellent optical properties. To reveal optical properties, the GQNF and AINF structures were systematically mapped with photoluminescence (PL), photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), and in situ confocal microscopy. Fig. 1d shows a significant improvement with the fluorescence enhancement over the transitional range of GQNF 5.0, i.e., GQNF 7.5 and AINF produce better optical emissions than other ratios, which are consistent with the preliminary hierarchical fiber splitting morphological patterns. Further, relating the concordance of GQNF 7.5 (7.63 ± 1.50 %) and AINF (12.13 ± 0.98 %) with PLQY enhancements, as depicted by Fig. 1e. When the threshold ratio of GQNF 7.5 is exceeded, the PL emission characteristics decline significantly and these results are largely correlated with aggregation factors. The term GQNF used hereafter will refer specifically to GQNF 7.5, as it demonstrates superior morphological and optical characteristics.

In order to interrelate the intriguing superior dispersion of AINF with hierarchical morphological aspects, in situ confocal studies help intuitively to understand optical emission micronanoscopically (Fig. 1f). Additionally, Fig. S3 unveils the dispersion of microscopic fibers and the behavior of beads formation over the excessive doping ratio. To the best of our knowledge, there is a critical niche in the easy formation of hierarchical angiogenesis inspired nanostructures that enable wearable electronics. A detailed explanation of the angiogenesis-inspired structures is attributed to electrospinning solution parameters, such as solution conductivity and surface tension properties. It is widely known that an improved solution conductivity in electrospinning will favor electrical fields that exists over the needle and collector zone, which mainly triggers angiogenesis inspired ultrafine nanofibers. Secondly, the surface tension characteristics of the resulting composite electrospinning solution initially determine the formation of the polymer jet. The initial formation of Taylor cones in the electrospinning jet favored by the contribution of surface tension, while the expansion of the jet with the oscillating electric field due to the solution conductivity synergistically supports the successful formation of ultrafine angiogenesis inspired nanofibers (Fig. 1g). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) included a gravimetric assessment of GQD fillers and surface modifier loading present in the GQNF and AINF structures (Fig. 1h). Intriguingly, Fig. S4 reveals the zoomed in TGA image, crucially linking the surface modifier loading ratio and thermal stability in the as-prepared AINF. Another important factor for robust nano-anchoring is the intrinsic chemical interaction between GQD fillers, surface modifier and cellulose matrix, which is systematically studied using Fourier Transformation Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The FTIR results revealing the existence of symmetrical and asymmetrical –CH2– stretching (2916 cm−1 and 2848 cm−1), –CH2– bending (1440–1470 cm−1), intensified acetyl peak shifts (1369 cm−1) strongly corroborate the constructive nano-anchoring physico-chemical supportive entanglement in AINF [41]. The broad peak observed between 3400 and 3600 cm−1 is indicative of O–H stretching vibrations, which suggest the presence of hydrogen-bonding interactions, particularly prominent in the AINF sample (Fig. S5). The enhanced intensity of this region in AINF, relative to GQNF and CNF, indicates a greater density of hydrogen bonding. This characteristic may significantly influence the material's structural stability [42]. The cumulative contribution of interesting robust morphologies and molecular interactions has improved the Ig/Id ratio of 1.10, suggesting a reduction in the defect state confirmed by Raman spectral analysis (Fig. S6) [43]. These molecular interactions and defects-passivating behaviors are well compatible and correlate with the pre-existing photoluminescent character elevation in the proposed GQNF and AINF structure. The tensile strength at break of AINF is 4.26 MPa, representing an approximate 20-fold improvement compared to the CNF sample, which has a strength of 0.21 MPa. This enhancement can be attributed to the effects of quantum confinement and nano-anchoring, as detailed in Table S1.

Where λ and ε corresponds to the tensile stress and strain at break. We determined the toughness values by analyzing the stress-strain plots of the prepared fibers. Additionally, the hierarchical GQNF and AINF exhibited significant improvements in mechanical toughness, with values approximately three and seven times greater (486.5 ± 3.48 kJm−3 and 967.2 ± 5.78 kJm− 3) than that of the CNF, which has a toughness of 143.3 ± 2.57 kJm− 3. These enhancements are due to synchronized fiber splitting and sub-nano regime entanglements, as shown in Fig. 1i. Notably, intermolecular bonding—specifically hydrogen bonds and π-π interactions—as well as the high dispersion of GQD fillers and the presence of numerous nano junctions contribute to physicochemical crosslinking, resulting in the highly robust mechanical structures of AINF that are well-suited for wearable electronic applications.

Evaluating the fluorescence lifetime proclaims the importance and restores successful quantum confinement with improved radiative recombination. Time resolved PL curves were fitted by the biexponential decay function as follows (Eq. (2) and (3)),

Where, F(t) is the time-dependent PL intensity;, and are the intensities at t = 0; and τ1,and τ2 are the non-radiative and radiative components of PL decay lifetime. On solving the Eq. (2), we deduce the A1. A2, τ1, and τ2 values to calculate the average lifetime using Eq. (3).

AINF shows a longer lifetime value of 11.52 ns compared to GQNF and is rationally related to the aggregation suppression effects offered by the optimized surface chemistry (Fig. 2 a). Furthermore, Table S2 suggests a significant improvement in the AINF radiative recombination factor due to the combined contributions provided by effective GQD nano-confinement, consistent with PL and PLQY results. Following the dispersion, emission performance and mechanical stability, it is equally important to achieve durability to reduce electronic waste and environmental pollution. Realizing the multifunctional materials derived from plastics and using natural polymers will lead to a circular economy and sustainable greener electronics. Environmental factors such as moisture, water and PBS solutions critically challenges the unperturbed performance of the wearable electronics. In the past, the perovskite emissive layers with mutual balance of PL stability and environmental stability are of great concern [5,44,45]. In this case, white light emitting GQD materials surpass the distinct green and red emissive layers, thereby reducing the materials cost, intensive work, fatigue loading and thickness optimization to achieve pure white light emission [46–48]. Benefiting from single layer emissive nano anchored AINF with enhanced optical and robust flexible optoelectronics, due to synergistic nano entanglements and inter adhered physicochemical interactions.

In order to meet real-time applications, it is essential to study the impact of ambient conditions such as moisture, water and PBS conditions. To investigate AINF's environmental stability behavior, AINF is submerged in water and PBS solution for 7 days. AINF slowly experience the degradation of optical emission, i.e., the PL emission retention is maintained at 49.22 ± 3.54 % and 21.34 ± 4.12 % after 7 days immersion in water and PBS solution, respectively (Fig. 2b and 2c). Because of the biocompatibility and non-toxicity of GQDs, there is no significant threat to the environment or humans. Following the stability trend, the AINF structures were successfully used in light-emitting diode applications by forming the device architecture described in Fig. 2d. After successfully verifying the above, the luminous properties of GQNF and AINF white light emitting diode (WLED) were observed, such as luminance, Commission Internationale de I’Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates, and electroluminescent (EL) spectra to unveil the EL and optical purity characteristics. Fig. 2e shows the AINF device's brightness curve with improved EL compared to the GQNF, and the inset shows the cool white light emission achieved through AINF devices, which signifies the importance of nano-reinforced GQDs and effectively encapsulated dispersion quality. Fig. 2f and 2 g suggest enhancements in color purity and color spectral purity, which are consistent with the initial PL and EL properties of the hierarchical AINF nanostructures. Specifically, AINF emits pure cool white light (0.31, 0.32) and GQNF emits CIE of (0.29, 0.32).

To further understand the purity of EL color provided by AINF, we carried out an EL spectral analysis that further supported the excellent coverage of visible wavelength range compared to GQNF devices (Fig. 2g). To test the degradation kinetics of CNF, GQNF, and AINF, the PBS immersion method applied presents insignificant deviations, as shown in Fig. 2h. In general, after 80 days, all fibrous systems degrade by more than 90 %, revealing their potential in vitro and in vivo studies. To evaluate the performance stability of the as-formed WLED devices, fatigue bend relaxation cycle tests were carried out on GQNF and AINF. Interestingly, the AINF luminance retention values (92.34 ± 0.21 %) are considerably higher than GQNFs (87.36 % ± 0.38 %) and such robust brightness retention characteristics are attributed to strongly inter-protruding nano-entangled robust engineered architectures, leading to flex-insensitive wearable optoelectronics (Fig. 2i). As wearable electronics tend to be in contact with human skin, it is desirable to have good cell viability and antibacterial properties. Fig. S7 & S8 results reveal the non-cytotoxic nature of GQNF and AINF, with an excellent cell viability of greater than 90 %, far above the recommendations of the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) [8]. Furthermore, the results of Fig. S9 & S10 show that the antibacterial properties of GQNF and AINF, with decent bacteriostatic performance against Escherichia Coli (E. Coli) and Staphylococcus Aureus (S. Aureus) over a seven-day period.

Considering the balanced mechanical, biodegradation, compatibility and antibacterial properties of the as-formed GQNF and AINF hierarchical structures, it is hypothesized to possess brighter potential in wearable personal protective equipment such as face masks and wearable smart sensors.

Highly motivated by the interpenetrating tortuous lightweight ultrafine nanofibrous achieved by GQNF and AINF, we aimed to solve the highly demanding disease prevention and transmission such as COVID-19, which falls within the range of 60–140 nm particle size [49]. Recent reports have revealed the respired aerosol of < 5 µm consists of SARS-CoV-2 RNA during the respiration and coughing activities and it is aerodynamic airborne transmission occur predominantly in the sub 300 nm size ranges [50,51]. By using GEODICT simulation software, three nanostructures were systematically studied by pre-defining the conditions of nanostructures such as diameter, surface area and thickness. Fig. 3 a (i) shows the nanostructure of the CNF, GQNF, and AINF membranes. A further review of filtration behavior shows that the three types of fibrous membranes with fixed predefined thickness show excellent characteristic filtration efficiency of GQNF and AINF, regardless of the diameter of the aerosol (Fig. 3a (ii)). In order to achieve higher quality factors in face masks, it is highly necessary to control the resistance of the airflow, i.e., reduce pressure drop. It is very difficult to balance filtration efficiency and pressure drop to achieve highly efficient quality factor-enabled face masks. Fig. 3a (iii) shows a ground breaking low-pressure drop of ∼ 6 Pa in the AINF, as a result of greater reduction in drag force and enhanced curvy structured slip effects. These preliminary simulation studies further challenge the existing achievement of quality factors in the manufacture of personal protective face masks. In addition, particle-size dependent filter performance simulation studies have motivated the manufacture of GQNF and AINF based COVID-19 face masks, which offer higher filtration performance above 100 nm particle diameter regimes (Fig. 3b). In particular, the filtration efficiency and pressure drop values of GQNF and AINF provide desirable real-time working values due to nanofiber split behavior, supportive entanglements and efficient adherence characteristics (Fig. 3c). Notably, the Geodict simulation software assists in understanding the importance of AINFs in achieving superior nanoporous filtration characteristics (Video S1). In general, the long-term use of face masks leads to breathing difficulties as the aerosol particles slowly accumulate and thereby undermine the breathing characteristics. To validate such breathability, a simulation-based filter stability test was carried out over 180 min to analyze the pressure drop retention characteristics of GQNF and AINF, indicating its reliability in the application of face masks (Fig. 3d). From the preliminary results of the face mask simulation, the GQNF and AINF structures would obtain beneficial results for human life by manufacturing highly efficient and easily breathable personal protective masks.

By implementing the first step optimization of the electrospinning nanofiber collection, it provides a simple control of the basis weight with the desired thickness (Fig. S11). Moreover, Fig. S12 & S13 shows the experimental configuration of the mask filter performance measurement, mask preparation, and we have adopted the standard National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) test procedure (more details are provided in the experimental section). In short, after optimizing the particle size, concentration of NaCl aerosol particle stream is allowed to pass through the as-designed filters. With the aid of aerosol particle counters, sol particle concentration is measured before and after the filtration process to calculate the filtration efficiency (%) by the Eq. (4),

Quality factor of the face masks can be systematically assessed with the as-obtained filtration efficiency and pressure drop values by applying the equation (6),

Fig. 3e shows the importance of the basis weight in controlling the balanced filtration efficiency PM0.2 (94.21 ± 1.48 %), pressure drop (17 ± 2.47 Pa) and quality factor (0.167 ± 0.01 Pa−1) of the AINF mask filters basis weight of 1.27 ± 0.12 gm−2. To assess breathing comfort, we measured the pressure drop of the commercial PM2.5 filter (315 Pa), which is 15 times greater than that of the proposed AINF face masks. Such breathing comfort was visualized using an infrared imaging camera, as illustrated in Fig. 3f. The elevated pressure drop reflects significant airflow resistance, potentially impacting user comfort. For our study, the airflow velocity was set at 85 Lmin-1, exceeding typical breathing rates. This configuration adheres to Darcy’s Law, which connects pressure drop to flow velocity in porous materials, providing a robust evaluation of the filter's performance and simulating real-world, high-demand conditions. In particular, the infrared thermal images of the AINFs are very uniform than those of the PM 2.5, which is directly related to easy breathing. Fig. 3g and 3 h summarizes the filtration efficiency, pressure drop, and quality factors of CNF, GQNF, and AINF by experimental and simulation conditions, respectively. Previous reports have suggested the manufacture of multi-hierarchical curvy and nanostructures of core shells and their efficacy in improving the quality factor in face masks compared to conventional structures [52,53]. Notably, dust particles produced by the real-time setup with the help of fuming incense sticks were recorded, and displayed to see the deposition of a wide range of smoke particles (Fig. S14). Taking further, Fig. S15 shows the bacterial filtration efficiency values, where AINF reaches an excellent filtration value of 99.9 ± 0.32 %. Excellence in effective trapping of NaCl aerosol particles using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) assessment clearly reveal the significant role of AINF filters (Fig. S16). Predominant role of AINF improved quality factor among the other two nanostructures indicate the importance of prepared nano-anchored highly robust hierarchical frameworks. Another possible contribution may be attributed to the surface potential values offered by multifunctional fillers, as shown in Fig. S17. Fig. 3i presents a real-time image of the innovative prototype facemask designed for protection against COVID-19 transmission. This facemask was developed as an integral component of our project aimed at improving protective efficiency. The design and materials of the prototype have been optimized to ensure significant defense against viral transmission, while also prioritizing functionality and user comfort. Interestingly, electrostatic contribution along with curvy nano-anchored sieving offered by the AINF tends to support the high quality ultrafine sol particle filtration even with ultralow thickness (213 ± 2.4 μm) and the EDX morphological aspects supports the effective surface distribution of NaCl sol particles (Fig. S18).

Optimal high-quality factors in CNF, GQNF and AINF face masks were used to study the effect of filtration efficiency and pressure drop stability. Fig. 3j and 3k results support the balanced preservation of durability and breathing and ventilation for the very efficient facemasks made. It is worth noting the increase in CNF filtration efficiency experience that occurs in the percolation of aerosol particles into membrane matrices, indicating the contribution of physical interception. Although face masks are entirely made of biodegradable materials, it is essential to reuse face masks through washing to excel in the cost-effective aspect. In order to mimic the real-time feasibility of highly efficient face masks, we have jointly studied the filtration efficiency of CNF, GQNF, and AINF face masks. Fig. 3l presents the results of the washing test, showing that AINF maintains a high filtration efficiency of 96.48 ± 1.43 % following multiple wash cycles. This remarkable durability is due to the mask’s excellent conformability and the densely entangled network of ultrafine nanofibers, which create a robust filtration structure. Such structural integrity ensures that the filtration efficiency remains consistently high, even with repeated use, thereby enhancing the mask's cost-effectiveness and longevity. AINF structures retained its filtration integrity even after three consecutive wash-dry cycles, which is technically verified with its morphological evolution after washing procedure (Fig. S19). To evaluate the soil degradation characteristics, the samples are permitted to degrade in the soil over designated time periods and are then gently washed to eliminate any soil residues. Following this, the samples are dried and weighed to obtain the weight (Wt). This procedure is conducted over a total duration of 80 days.

Environmental soil degradation test results suggests 88.45 ± 8.35 % reduction of its initial mass after 80 days under laboratory simulated conditions (Fig. S20 & Eq. (7)). Table S3 contrast the face mask filter performance with other recently reported literatures in terms of filtration efficiency, pressure drop, quality factor and degradability of the as-prepared AINFs.

Fig. 4 a represents the angiogenesis inspired nanofiber based piezoelectric energy generator device architecture, where CNF/ silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) acts as an air permeable flexible electrode. Optimized in situ formation of AgNPs using eco-friendly L-ascorbic acid reducing agent led to the development of highly percolating conductive air permeable electrode with an ultralow sheet resistance of 3.74 ± 0.08 ΩSq− 1. Formation of percolative conductive interpenetrating nano channels were verified with Fig. S21. Interestingly, successful optimization of flexible electrode qualifies the flexing endurance test of 1000 cycles with very negligible deviations in electrical performance proving its flex-insensitive conductive electrode behavior (Fig. S22). Such flex-insensitive durable electrode can be of greater significance for wearable piezoelectric energy generator fabrication. Such structures with sandwich accommodation of AINF structures will lead to highly flexible, sensitive, and wearable theranostics. Taking these into account, we initially assessed the piezoelectric voltage comparison among the CNF (5.01 ± 1.12 V), GQNF (17.20 ± 1.11 V), and AINF (34.84 ± 1.36 V) based energy generators, which can be conveniently termed as CNG, GQNG, and AING due to its piezoelectric energy generating capability (Fig. 4b). It is reasonable to compare the results of the piezoelectric harvester with the well-known commercial piezoelectric synthetic polymer polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) and, more interestingly, AING devices perform far better than other devices. Furthermore, piezoelectric mechanistic verification is performed using the bias reversal procedure of the AING device, since the polarization of the dipoles reverses with the change of the direction of the impact (Fig. 4c). To fully understand the piezoelectric response behavior, we studied frequency-dependent variations ranging from 1 Hz to 4 Hz at fixed impact conditions, which critically simulates real-time working conditions (Fig. 4d). To understand the improvement in the piezoelectric behavior of AING, we used the piezoelectric force microscope (PFM) to initially verify morphology and phase verification. The preliminary results of the PFM 3D height profile and narrow phase angle distribution trends are consistent with the initial SEM fiber morphology and TEM filler dispersion observations obtained by GQNF and AINF (Fig. S23).

Apart from piezoelectric voltage generation, it is of critical significance to understand the piezoelectric coefficient values for wearable theranostics since it offers greater sensitivity with respect to human motions. PFM butterfly loop studies piezoelectric switching behavior of fibrous structures with respect to applied electrical bias following the inverse piezoelectric effects. Consistent higher outputs of AINF amplitude versus bias voltage and the deduced d33 values reveals the synergistic contribution from nano-anchored structures and crystallization contribution of cellulosic material (Fig. S24). To further understand the crystallization and crystalline orientation of the as prepared cellulose derived hierarchical nanostructures, we utilized the X-ray diffraction studies (Fig. S25), which revealed the significant elevation in the crystallinic characteristics of AINF structures (consistent with FTIR and Raman results). We conjecture that higher d33 values are ascribed to the crystallinic alignments in the cellulosic nano entangled hierarchical structures and the resulting 2D wide-angle X-ray spectroscopy (2D WAXS) outputs clearly revealing the greater crystallinic alignment with our champion AINF based nanogenerators (Fig. S26). The 2D WAXS peaks were utilized to determine the degree of alignment (π) using the following equation.
(8)
π =
180 - F W H M
180
Where, FWHM refers to the full width half maximum of the highly intense azimuthal peaks obtained in the 2D WAXS results. Eq. (8) has been employed to quantify crystalline orientations, and these comprehensive results (0.95 close to unity) validate the performance enhancements observed in our champion AING device. Stemming from the excellent piezoelectric coefficient of 2694 pmV−1 and the extremely robust nano-anchored crystalline alignment cohesively contribute to efficient stress transfer (Fig. S27). The proposed AING exhibits significant durability, successfully enduring 15,000 cycles while achieving a retention rate of 97.85 % in performance (Fig. 4e). This exceptional level of durability indicates that the material can uphold its functional integrity over extended use, making it appropriate for wearable theranostic applications. Ensuring long-term storage stability is crucial for the successful commercialization of wearable electronics. The proposed material demonstrates a retention of 92.15 % of its performance when stored at room temperature and 70 % relative humidity for six months (Fig. 4f). This highlights its durability and suitability for practical applications that demand reliable performance over time in wearable technologies. Table S4 provides a comparison of the state-of-the-art results for piezoelectric materials, along with their respective figures of merit. Further, the AING self-powering ability validated by COMSOL piezoelectric voltage (Fig. S28) and the results in line with experimental studies led to the development of effective and self-powered post-anesthetic tremor diagnostics with distinctive electrical responses (Videos S2).

Encouraging results from the AING device further led to the in vitro cell studies. The cell proliferation process was monitored by capturing the optical images at the specified intervals of time. Eq. (9) aids in calculating the cell proliferation rates after 24 and 48 h.

Fig. 4g suggests that AING supported the acceleration of cell proliferation behavior of the proposed AING structures. Fig. S29 results also support the phenomenon of cell migration after self-powered piezoelectric stimulation using an AING device, imitating the scenario of real-time injury healing under laboratory operational conditions. Fig. 4h illustrates the significant cell proliferation rate of the AING, reaching approximately 3.72 times that of control samples. This remarkable outcome is attributed to the integrated self-powered electrical contributions of the material. This elevated cellular activity underscores the potential of AING in facilitating rapid tissue growth, which is a critical characteristic for regenerative applications.

To further assess its in vitro healing potential, a migration test was performed by creating an artificial scratch in a petri dish and observing cell movement into the scratched area through optical microscopy. Initial images of the scratched regions were captured, and cell migration was monitored at specific time intervals. Eq. (10) was utilized to calculate the accelerated cell migration rates at 24, 48, 72, and 96 h, offering quantitative insights into the influence of AING on cellular dynamics.

Fig. 4i quantitatively demonstrates that AING exhibits a migration rate 2.61 times faster than that of control samples. This enhanced migration ability highlights AING's effectiveness in facilitating cellular repair and wound healing in a controlled laboratory setting. Collectively, these findings validate AING's superior performance in promoting both cell proliferation and migration, positioning it as a highly promising material for tissue regeneration and wound healing applications.

As evidenced by the biocompatible and biodegradable nature of the as-fabricated self-powered air permeable nanogenerators, we intend to fabricate the therapeutic electronic skin-based tissue regeneration with an accelerated rate of skin tissue proliferation, which would be beneficial for chronic ulcers tissue reconstruction. To date, research trends have shown several successful therapeutic protocols such as traditional wound treatments, adhesive bands, nanoparticle-infused components, ultrasonication, photomodulation and applied electric field therapy. Nevertheless, wearability, comfort and real-time applicability, such as the installation-removal of the device with the skill-free low-end civilian operation, remain challenging. In order to address the above-mentioned challenges, it is believed that self-powered sustainable AING devices with improved wearability and mechanical robustness can significantly surpass other wound healing strategies in terms of energy consumption, scalability, processing and easy adoption (Fig. 5 a). To unravel the contribution of the AING device to chronic thick-walled and large-area healing with minimal intervention, the dorsal part of Sprague Dawley rats (SD) was chosen as a model of excision wound healing (initial diameter 25 mm). The wound area of each sample was monitored at specific intervals using digital photography, and the area was quantified with ImageJ software for accurate measurement.

Based on Eq. (11) and the resulting data, Fig. 5b and S30 provide a comparative analysis of chronic large-area wound recovery among control samples, CNG, and AING over a 28-day period. It is noteworthy that AING exhibits significantly improved wound healing, achieving a substantial recovery of 79.28 % ± 4.21 % within just 14 days. Furthermore, AING treatment facilitates considerable hair regrowth in the wound area, doubling the amount observed in the control samples. After 28 days, AING showed the best wound healing efficiency of 98.75 ± 0.74 %. Such near 100 % efficiency seems to be more effective than that of control groups (72.10 ± 1.39 %) attributable to the inbuilt electric field self-powered highly adhered angiogenesis inspired structure mimicking the extracellular matrix that facilitates the formation of an appropriate environment to aid the accelerated tissue regeneration.

The proposed AING device in-vivo animal model serves instrumental in achieving the simple and straightforward to harvest the highly efficient real time wound healing efficiency. To study the evolution of the proliferative phase, it is essential to analyze the recovered injury area with a histological examination. After chronic wound recovery, newly formed skin tissues were extracted, and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson’s trichrome staining protocols. In particular, the H&E results the clear distinctive reconstruction pattern in terms of CNG and AING case. Fig. 5c shows vividly the formation of an intact thick epidermal layer and a new blood vessel around the CNG and AING dye profiles, compared to the control case, which means the effective contribution of nano-entangled highly adhered air permeable robust nanostructures. The control results for the stains are perfectly in line with the observation of the healing of the real wounds (as shown in Fig. 5b) with the existence of the scab. In addition to the above, it is highly desirable to know the formation of collagen deposits around the area of regeneration. Availing the Masson’s trichrome staining technique, verification of highly dense collagen protein formation in the tissue remodeling phase is constructively observed in the order of control < CNG < AING (Fig. 5d). To quantify precisely, the imageJ software supported analysis significantly revealed AING’s acceleration of the healing and reconstruction process of skin tissue with an intact epidermal layer development of 138.34 ± 16.47 μm, whereas control exerts minimal recovery of 7.12 ± 1.65 μm (Fig. 5e). Intriguingly, AING device assisted tissue regrowth and hair follicle growth factors analyzed by Masson trichrome dyeing resulted in greater agreement with the above skin epidermal tissue reformation by accelerating the growth phase evidenced from the intense green color collagen deposits (Fig. 5f & 5 g). In addition, hair follicle counts assessments therefore support the formation of angiogenesis, i.e., newly generated blood vessels and such antibacterial and accelerated therapeutic effects highlights the AING significance. Overall, AING's minimally invasive, careless wound treatment model successfully met real-time significance, and its effectiveness was verified by statistically insignificant variations between control samples. The proposed win–win strategy enabled carefree AING theranostics applied effectively with better-balanced wearability, comfort, conformity, ease of maintenance and self-powering sustainable textile characteristics without compromising cost and operational stability.

In summary, we have successfully developed a win–win strategy that enables economically viable and environmentally friendly AINF-based personal protective theranostic textiles with high throughput and biodegradable characteristics to achieve greener and more sustainable energy-based environments. The proper optimization of the compatible filler ratio, diffusion and conformability led to the manufacture of flexible, non-sensitive wearable WLEDs. Due to the hierarchical chemical and physical interactions in the hierarchical frameworks of the mechanically robust nano-entangled AINF based biodegradable masks with outstanding filtration efficiency PM0.2 ∼ 94 %, pressure drop ∼ 17 Pa and quality factor ∼ 0.167 Pa−1 have therefore been produced under an airflow of 85 Lmin−1. In addition, we have demonstrated nano-anchored cellulosic structures of AING along with higher degree of crystallinic alignments that favor the higher piezoelectric coefficient of 2694 pmV−1. Furthermore, AING's self-powered characteristics is systematically carried out and successfully extended to in vivo tremor diagnostics and chronic, large-scale subcutaneous wound tissue regeneration therapy. In short, the proposed AING could be a game changer in combating global warming, plastic pollution and the energy crisis by acting as an effective barrier to highly transmissive COVID-19 viruses and next-generation smart personal skin care textiles.

All the commercial materials purchased and utilized without any further purification. Chemicals and its corresponding details are presented with parenthesis: cellulose acetate(Mn 30000, Sigma-Aldrich), dimethyl acetamide (DMAc) (99 %, Tedia), acetone (99 %) silver trifluoroacetate (98 %, combi-blocks), L-ascorbic acid (99 %, Sigma-Aldrich), polyethylene terephthalate (PET) (locally recycled), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (99 %, Acros organics), deionized water, ethanol (99.8 %, Honey well), sodium chloride (99 %, Sigma-Aldrich), Incense (local store), foetal bovine serum (FBS) (biological industries), Sodium phosphate monobasic (NaH2PO4) and sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4) were used for the preparation of phosphate buffer solution (PBS), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM; Gibco, Gaithersburg, MD, USA), L929 cells (mouse fibroblast, ATCC, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of microplasma based white emitting graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: PET was subjected to microplasma for 5 min at ambient conditions to synthesize the white-emitting GQDs. The microplasma was occurred by applying 2 kV, 10 mA power on the stainless tube with ID 180 mm as the cathode and platinum as the anode. The 150 kΩ resistor in series with power supply is to limit the current and raise the voltage to breakdown argon to form microplasma. 0.1 M NaOH was used as electrolyte to form the current loop. The obtained crude solution is washed with DI water and filtered sequentially and finally dried into GQD powder.

Cellulose acetatepolymer solution(15 wt%) made with solvent mixture ratio of 2:1 acetone and DMAc, respectively. The resultant solution is allowed to stir overnight with the aid of magnetic stirrer. Electrospinning of the cellulose acetate polymer using the metallic needle biased at 13 kVvoltage supplywith the optimized flow rate of 0.8 mLhr−31 and distance of 15 cm, yields the CNF. Theentire electrospinning was carried out at room temperature and relative humidity 50–70 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cellulose-graphene quantum dot composite nanofibers (GQNF)
ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose acetatepolymer solution(15 wt%) and graphene quantum dots (x = 2.5. 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5 wt%) made with solvent mixture ratio of 2:1 acetone and DMAc, respectively. The resultant solution is subject to thorough mixing via room temperature magnetic stirring. These resulting polymeric composite solution were electrospun with the following conditions; 13–15 kV voltage, 0.8–1.0 mLhr−31 and distance 15 cm, yields the xGQNF (x = 2.5. 5.0, 7.5, 10.0, 12.5). Theentire electrospinning was carried out at room temperature and relative humidity 40–60 %.

Cellulose acetatepolymer solutionof 15 wt%, optimized graphene quantum dots of 7.5 wt%, and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (2 wt%) made with solvent mixture ratio of 2:1 acetone and DMAc, respectively. The resultant solution is subject to thorough mixing via room temperature magnetic stirring. These resulting polymeric composite solution were electrospun with the following conditions; 15 kV voltage, 0.5–0.7 mLhr−31 and distance 15 cm, yields the AINF structures. Theentire electrospinning was carried out at room temperature and relative humidity 50–70 %. It is worth mentioning that electrospinning collection time was optimized to achieve the desired thickness and basis weight for specific applications.

The mask filtration filters are made of sustainable tri-layered structures, following the order of traditional cotton fabric- CNF/ GQNF/ AINF/ traditional cotton fabric, i.e, the active eco-friendly biodegradable nanofibrous component is sandwiched in between the biodegradable natural cotton woven structures. The mask testing was conducted according to the modified National Institute of occupational Safety and Health NIOSH (42 CFR Part 84–2019) test standards with NaCl sol particles and the detailed parameters were furnished in the following section.

All the testing fiber samples were preconditioned at 85 % RH and 38 ℃ for atleast 24 h. Filtration test was performed using an atomizer (TSI Inc., USA) which generates sodium chloride (NaCl) aerosol having multi-particle size distributions of 30–500 nm. The NaCl aerosols were subjected to a charge neutralizer to produce neutral charge aerosols and it’s mixing ratio and flow rate (85 Lmin−1) is controlled with the aid of mass flow controller (Protec, PC-540). The NaCl sol particles were counted with scanning mobility particle sizer (TSI Inc., USA) before admitting the sol particles into the test filter chamber. After penetrating the testing samples, the sol particles were counted with similar scanning mobility particle sizer unit to calculate the filtration efficiency. Similarly, the pressure drop were calculated using differential pressure transmitter (PEL, 1131110). Using the filtration efficiency and pressure drop, quality factor values were deduced to evaluate the total mask filter performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of air permeable conductive electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CNF structures were sequentially dip coated with ethanolic solution of silver trifluoroacetate (15 wt%) solution for a time period of 5 mins, and further it is reduced with eco-friendly reducing agent ascorbic acid solution (1:1 v/v%) to generate the conductive in situ AgNPs. The residual reducing agents were removed with deionized water washing to yield the air permeable electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of piezoelectric nanogenerators (CNG, GQNG and AING)
ce:section.ce:para: The as-obtained optimized CNF, GQNF and AINF structures were selectively employed with conductive air permeable electrodes to establish the electrical external circuits yielding the fully fibrous piezoelectric energy harvester. Furthermore, the piezoelectric behavior verification with forward and reverse bias connections yielding the obvious voltage signal shifts. Depending on the piezoelectric active component selection such as CNF, GQNF and AINF, it is successively termed as CNF, GQNG and AING (where G represents the energy generators). The entire processing conditions remains the same for all the three generators despite the changes in the piezoelectric active fibers.

The morphologies of the fibers were characterized using field emission scanning electron microscopy (JEOL JSM-6510), scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S-520), transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Philips Tecnai F30 with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV and FEI Tecnai TM G2 F-20 S-TWIN with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV), confocal fluorescent microscopy (Leica TCS SP5) and Optical Microscopy (Nikon Eclipse, 50i, Japan). Samples were coated with platinum prior toSEMcharacterization, and the analysis was performed at an acceleration rate of 15 kV. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were performed using a a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were obtained using a Research Grade Spectrofluorometer system (FP-8500, JASCO). The TR-PL spectra were coupled to a spectrometer (Horiba iHR320) with a Hamamatsu C10910 streak camera and an M10913 slow single sweep unit. Solution conductivity was measured using conductivity meter (iSTEK K5000-CP). Contact angle and surface tension measurements were made using contact angle goniometer (Sindatek 100SB). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted using a TA Instruments Q50 instrument with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from 100 to 800 °C under a flow of nitrogen. A tensile test QC 508 (Cometech Testing Machines Co., Ltd., Taiwan) was conducted for determining the mechanical properties. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Bio-Rad 155 FT-IR spectrometer at ambient temperature in the range of 400 − 4000 cm−1. The XRD patterns of the prepared films were characterized by PANalytical diffractometer (X’ Pert3 Powder). The GIWAXS and GISAXS patterns were conducted on beamline BL23A1 in the National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC), Taiwan. The conversion from the scattering angle (θ) to the scattering vector (q) is based on the formula, Q = 4π/λsinθ, where λ is the X-ray wavelength. The luminance and Commission Internationale de l′éclairage (CIE) color coordinates of the LEC devices were measured by Keithley source and PR670 spectroradiometer (Titan Electro-Optics Co., Ltd., Taiwan).Capacitance measurement was performed using Agilent 4824A precision LCR meter and dielectric constant is derived using the standard equation. Piezoelectric responses and hysteresis loops were obtained through probe force microscopy (PFM, Bruker icon with scanasyst) by utilizing a Pt/Ir-coated silicon-based conductive AFM tip.Surface potential measurements were done with the help of Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM, Bruker icon with scanasyst). Mechanical impact on the nanogenerator devices were tuned with different frequency to obtain the piezoelectric outputs using GW INSTEK GDS-2072E oscilloscope and Agilent 4824A precision LCR meter. The sheet resistance of the electrode was measured using a four-point probe and a Keithley 2400 source meter.The thermal images of the masks were measured by Thermal Imaging Camera (FLIR one pro).COMSOL multiphysics version of 5.2a deployed to analyze the load dependent displacements and piezoelectric potential. Geodict simulation software designed with Fibergeo model (version 2022) were used to calculate the filtration efficiency, pressure drop and filtration stability. MicroRaman measurements were conducted at room temperature with a JASCO 5100 spectrometer with green laser excitation of 532 nm. The Raman spectrometer was calibrated before the measurement using a Si wafer with a Raman shifts of 520 cm−1.
The remaining experimental details, equations & its corresponding characterization are furnished in the supporting information.
